agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"A courier-tracking feature where customers can view their courier's real-time location during delivery. Two internal stakeholder teams have issued directly contradictory non-negotiable requirements: Customer Success demands full GPS transparency, Operations demands zero location sharing to protect driver retention.","bounded_context":"Delivery Tracking / Order Fulfillment","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Courier","definition":"A delivery driver employed or contracted by the platform who picks up and delivers packages to customers."},{"term":"Real-time Location","definition":"The courier's GPS coordinates as reported by their device during an active delivery."},{"term":"Active Delivery","definition":"The period between a courier picking up a package and completing the drop-off to the customer."},{"term":"Delivery Transparency","definition":"The degree to which order progress and courier position are visible to the end customer."},{"term":"Driver Retention","definition":"The platform's ability to keep couriers actively working on the platform over time, a critical supply-side metric."},{"term":"Location Granularity","definition":"The level of precision at which a courier's position is shared — ranging from exact GPS coordinates to zone-level approximation to ETA-only."}]},"business_rules":[],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Courier is stationary for extended period (e.g., stuck in traffic, on break) — exact GPS exposes this and creates customer complaints AND driver discomfort simultaneously.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until contradiction is resolved. This edge case amplifies the tension between both positions."},{"scenario":"Courier is near customer's address but delivering another order first (multi-stop route) — exact GPS shows courier 'right there' but not arriving, causing frustration.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define until contradiction is resolved. This is a known source of customer complaints that exact GPS makes worse, not better."},{"scenario":"Courier is in a sensitive location (hospital, shelter, residential area between deliveries) — GPS sharing may create privacy or safety concerns beyond driver sentiment.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define — may have legal/regulatory implications (GDPR, local labor privacy laws) that neither team has raised."},{"scenario":"Customer uses location data to intercept courier en route rather than waiting at delivery address — safety concern for drivers.","expected_behavior":"Cannot define. This is a documented safety risk in the courier industry that strengthens Operations' concern."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"HIGH — This decision directly affects both sides of a two-sided marketplace. Losing drivers (supply side) increases delivery costs, extends delivery times, and caps growth. Losing customer satisfaction (demand side) reduces order volume and lifetime value. Getting this wrong on either side is existential. A compromise that satisfies neither side is also a financial risk.","operational":"SEVERE — If drivers leave due to surveillance concerns, operational capacity drops, remaining drivers are overloaded, SLAs degrade, and a vicious cycle begins. Conversely, if customers feel blind about their delivery, support ticket volume increases and NPS drops.","user":"Two distinct user groups with opposing needs. Customers want certainty their delivery is coming. Couriers want dignity and privacy while working. Both are legitimate. Framing this as 'transparency' vs 'privacy' is a false binary — the real user need on the customer side is delivery confidence, not GPS coordinates.","risk":"CRITICAL — Building either team's requirement as stated will actively harm the other team's core metric. There is also an unaddressed legal/regulatory risk: sharing worker GPS coordinates with third parties may violate labor privacy regulations in multiple jurisdictions (GDPR Article 88, various US state biometric/location privacy laws). No legal review has been cited by either team.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly affected — they want delivery visibility and confidence that their order is arriving."},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"Directly affected — their real-time location is the data in question. Exact GPS sharing creates a surveillance dynamic that is driving attrition."},{"group":"Customer Success Team","impact":"Owns customer satisfaction metrics. Believes GPS transparency drives NPS and differentiation."},{"group":"Operations Team","impact":"Owns driver supply and retention. Has empirical evidence (driver complaints, attrition data) that GPS sharing is harmful."},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"NOT YET CONSULTED — sharing worker location data with customers has regulatory implications that neither team has addressed."},{"group":"Product/Executive Leadership","impact":"Must arbitrate this conflict. This is a strategic product decision, not a feature spec decision."}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"Confidence is very low because there is a direct, unresolved contradiction between two authoritative stakeholder teams, both presenting their position as non-negotiable. Additionally, there is a completely unaddressed legal/regulatory dimension. No executive arbitration has occurred. No user research has been cited by either side to support their position. I cannot define correct acceptance criteria when the definition of 'correct' is actively contested. The spec is not implementable as stated.","high_confidence_areas":["This is a genuine business contradiction, not a misunderstanding — both teams are talking about the same feature with opposite requirements","The real customer need is delivery confidence, not necessarily raw GPS data","Driver privacy and retention is a legitimate operational concern with real cost implications","There is an unaddressed legal/regulatory dimension"],"low_confidence_areas":["Which business priority should win — this requires executive arbitration, not domain analysis","Whether a compromise (e.g., zone-level tracking, ETA-only) would satisfy either team","The actual magnitude of driver attrition caused by GPS sharing vs other factors","Regulatory exposure in the platform's operating jurisdictions"],"assumptions":["Both teams are acting in good faith and representing real stakeholder concerns","Operations team's claim about driver attrition is based on actual data, not speculation","Customer Success team's claim about transparency as a differentiator is based on market analysis","No executive decision or company policy already exists that resolves this conflict"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"Customer Success requires exact GPS coordinates shown to customers at all times during delivery. Operations requires courier location to NEVER be shown to customers. These are mutually exclusive requirements from two authoritative internal teams, both stated as non-negotiable. This cannot be resolved at the domain analysis level — it requires executive arbitration on business strategy.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Couriers","Customer Success Team","Operations Team","Executive Leadership"],"options":["A: Side with Customer Success — show exact GPS. Accept driver attrition risk.","B: Side with Operations — show no location. Accept customer satisfaction risk.","C: Find a middle ground — show approximate location (zone, street-level, or progress-bar) that gives customers confidence without creating a surveillance dynamic for drivers. This reframes the requirement from 'GPS coordinates' to 'delivery confidence.'","D: Make it driver-controlled — let couriers opt in/out of location sharing, with appropriate customer messaging."],"recommendation":"Option C. Neither team's stated requirement reflects the actual underlying need. Customers need delivery confidence, not a GPS feed. Drivers need to not feel surveilled, not necessarily total invisibility. A zone-level or ETA-based tracking approach (similar to what Uber evolved toward after similar driver concerns) likely satisfies both underlying needs. However, THIS IS A STRATEGIC PRODUCT DECISION that must be made by executive leadership after consulting Legal, not by an engineering team. I strongly recommend: (1) Executive arbitration meeting with both teams, (2) Legal review of location-sharing obligations and constraints, (3) User research with BOTH customers and drivers on acceptable tracking granularity, before any implementation begins."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No legal or regulatory review has been conducted on sharing courier GPS data with customers. In many jurisdictions, sharing worker location data with third parties has specific regulatory requirements or prohibitions. This is not optional context — it could make one or both positions legally untenable.","affected_stakeholders":["Legal/Compliance","Couriers","Executive Leadership"],"options":["Proceed without legal review and accept regulatory risk","Block feature development until legal review is complete"],"recommendation":"Block feature development until legal review is complete. Regulatory violations in this space carry significant fines and reputational damage."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Direct contradiction between two authoritative stakeholder requirements makes the spec unimplementable as stated.","business_rule_broken":"Cannot define business rules when the fundamental business intent is contested. Customer Success says 'always show exact GPS.' Operations says 'never show location.' Both cannot be encoded."},{"violation":"No legal/regulatory review has been conducted on sharing worker location data with customers.","business_rule_broken":"Any location-sharing business rule must be validated against applicable privacy regulations before it can be treated as a requirement."}]}
